text: Git branching is an essential part of version control workflows. Developers create feature branches to work on new features or bug fixes without affecting the main codebase. Once work on a branch is complete, it is merged back into the main branch, often called "main" or "master." Before merging, developers typically submit pull requests, which allow team members to review the code changes and discuss improvements. This process ensures that only tested and approved changes are integrated. By using branches, teams can collaborate efficiently, keep the main code stable, and maintain a clear history of changes in the project.
